texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Theory and a Transferable Router for Two-Hop QA
texts.text: Theory and a Transferable Router for Two-Hop QA

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Two-hop QA retrieval splits queries into two regimes determined by whether the hop-2 entity is explicitly named in the question (Qdominant) or only in the bridge passage (Bdominant). We formalize this split with three theorems: (T1) per-query AUC is a monotone function of the cosine separation margin, with R 2 ≥ 0 . 90 for six of eight type × encoder pairs; (T2) regime is characterized by two surface-text predicates, with P1 decisive for routing and P2 qualifying the B-dominant case, validated across three encoders and three datasets; (T3) bridge advantage requires the relation-bearing sentence B rel, not entity name alone, since removing it collapses performance by 8.6-14.1 pp ( p < 0 . 001 ). Building on this theory we propose REGIMEROUTER, a lightweight binary router that selects between question-only and question + B rel retrieval using five text features derived directly from the predicate definitions. Trained on 2WikiMultiHopQA (n=881, 5-fold cross-fitted) and applied zero-shot to MuSiQue and HotpotQA, REGIMEROUTER achieves ∆ R @5 of +5 . 6 pp ( p < 0 . 001 ), +5 . 3 pp ( p = 0 . 002 ), and +1 . 1 pp (ns, no-regret) respectively, with a single frozen deployment rule: α = 0 . 25 across all three datasets ( α = 0 . 5 reported separately as an in-domain ablation upper bound). Human annotation (Cohen's κ = 1 . 00 (Cohen, 1960), n = 50 ) and replication across NV-Embed-v2, BGE-large, and e5-mistral confirm all findings are structural, not artifact.
texts.text: Two-hop QA retrieval splits queries into two regimes determined by whether the hop-2 entity is explicitly named in the question (Qdominant) or only in the bridge passage (Bdominant). We formalize this split with three theorems: (T1) per-query AUC is a monotone function of the cosine separation margin, with R 2 ≥ 0 . 90 for six of eight type × encoder pairs; (T2) regime is characterized by two surface-text predicates, with P1 decisive for routing and P2 qualifying the B-dominant case, validated across three encoders and three datasets; (T3) bridge advantage requires the relation-bearing sentence B rel, not entity name alone, since removing it collapses performance by 8.6-14.1 pp ( p < 0 . 001 ). Building on this theory we propose REGIMEROUTER, a lightweight binary router that selects between question-only and question + B rel retrieval using five text features derived directly from the predicate definitions. Trained on 2WikiMultiHopQA (n=881, 5-fold cross-fitted) and applied zero-shot to MuSiQue and HotpotQA, REGIMEROUTER achieves ∆ R @5 of +5 . 6 pp ( p < 0 . 001 ), +5 . 3 pp ( p = 0 . 002 ), and +1 . 1 pp (ns, no-regret) respectively, with a single frozen deployment rule: α = 0 . 25 across all three datasets ( α = 0 . 5 reported separately as an in-domain ablation upper bound). Human annotation (Cohen's κ = 1 . 00 (Cohen, 1960), n = 50 ) and replication across NV-Embed-v2, BGE-large, and e5-mistral confirm all findings are structural, not artifact.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Multi-hop retrieval systems routinely treat the bridge passage as a second query: encode the bridge in query mode, re-retrieve from the corpus, and fuse the results. This design implicitly assumes that the bridge passage contains retrievaluseful information beyond what the original question encodes.
texts.text: Multi-hop retrieval systems routinely treat the bridge passage as a second query: encode the bridge in query mode, re-retrieve from the corpus, and fuse the results. This design implicitly assumes that the bridge passage contains retrievaluseful information beyond what the original question encodes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We show that assumption is correct for some queries and incorrect for others , and that the split is predictable from surface text.
texts.text: We show that assumption is correct for some queries and incorrect for others , and that the split is predictable from surface text.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Consider a comparison question: 'Which of Person A or Person B was born earlier?' The question explicitly names both hop-2 candidates. A bi-encoder trained on (question, gold-passage) pairs embeds this question near passages about Person A and Person B, since both targets are already named in the question. The bridge passage (about one of them) adds no disambiguation the question has not already provided. Dense reencoding of the bridge is redundant; the question embedding alone ranks the correct passage first. This is the Q-dominant regime : P1=True (both answer entities appear in the question).
texts.text: Consider a comparison question: 'Which of Person A or Person B was born earlier?' The question explicitly names both hop-2 candidates. A bi-encoder trained on (question, gold-passage) pairs embeds this question near passages about Person A and Person B, since both targets are already named in the question. The bridge passage (about one of them) adds no disambiguation the question has not already provided. Dense reencoding of the bridge is redundant; the question embedding alone ranks the correct passage first. This is the Q-dominant regime : P1=True (both answer entities appear in the question).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Now consider a compositional question: 'What nationality is the director of Film X?' The question refers to the hop-2 entity only indirectly, as 'the director of Film X' , never by name. The hop-1 bridge passage about Film X contains the sentence 'Film X was directed by Person Y' : a relation-bearing sentence that names Person Y explicitly. Without this sentence, no embedding knows to retrieve Person Y's nationality page. With it, fusing the bridge sentence into the retrieval score corrects the ranking. This is the Bdominant regime : P1=False (the answer entity is not named in the question), P2=True (it is named in the bridge).
texts.text: Now consider a compositional question: 'What nationality is the director of Film X?' The question refers to the hop-2 entity only indirectly, as 'the director of Film X' , never by name. The hop-1 bridge passage about Film X contains the sentence 'Film X was directed by Person Y' : a relation-bearing sentence that names Person Y explicitly. Without this sentence, no embedding knows to retrieve Person Y's nationality page. With it, fusing the bridge sentence into the retrieval score corrects the ranking. This is the Bdominant regime : P1=False (the answer entity is not named in the question), P2=True (it is named in the bridge).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We formalize this dichotomy as two retrieval regimes (Section 3), prove three theorems characterizing their structure (Section 4), and build a practical transferable router exploiting the theory (Sections 5-6).
texts.text: We formalize this dichotomy as two retrieval regimes (Section 3), prove three theorems characterizing their structure (Section 4), and build a practical transferable router exploiting the theory (Sections 5-6).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. Regime theory (T1-T3): the retrieval outcome of any two-hop query is characterized by two binary surface-text predicates: P1
texts.text: Regime theory (T1-T3): the retrieval outcome of any two-hop query is characterized by two binary surface-text predicates: P1

texts.label: text
texts.orig: is decisive for routing; P2 qualifies the Bdominant case when P1 is false. Validated across three encoders and three datasets.
texts.text: is decisive for routing; P2 qualifies the Bdominant case when P1 is false. Validated across three encoders and three datasets.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. REGIMEROUTER : a five-feature binary router trained on predicate proxies, requiring no oracle labels and no embeddings at inference time; routing decisions use only surfacetext features.
texts.text: REGIMEROUTER : a five-feature binary router trained on predicate proxies, requiring no oracle labels and no embeddings at inference time; routing decisions use only surfacetext features.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 3. Cross-dataset no-regret : significant gain on MuSiQue ( p = 0 . 002 , zero-shot) and positive non-significant transfer on HotpotQA, with a single frozen deployment rule for α .
texts.text: Cross-dataset no-regret : significant gain on MuSiQue ( p = 0 . 002 , zero-shot) and positive non-significant transfer on HotpotQA, with a single frozen deployment rule for α .

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 4. Structural validation : bridge knockout experiment (T3), pool invariance (T1 attack surface), and human annotation ( κ = 1 . 00 ) rule out artifact explanations.
texts.text: Structural validation : bridge knockout experiment (T3), pool invariance (T1 attack surface), and human annotation ( κ = 1 . 00 ) rule out artifact explanations.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Multi-hop retrieval. Given a two-hop question q and corpus C , the task is to return the topk passages under R @ k , where both a bridge passage g 1 and a gold passage g 2 must appear in the retrieved set. Dominant methods iterate: retrieve g 1 (hop1), then use g 1 to generate or retrieve g 2 (hop-2).
texts.text: Multi-hop retrieval. Given a two-hop question q and corpus C , the task is to return the topk passages under R @ k , where both a bridge passage g 1 and a gold passage g 2 must appear in the retrieved set. Dominant methods iterate: retrieve g 1 (hop1), then use g 1 to generate or retrieve g 2 (hop-2).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Asymmetric bi-encoders. Modern retrievers like NV-Embed-v2 (Lee et al., 2024), BGElarge (Xiao et al., 2023), and e5-mistral (Wang et al., 2024) use separate encoder modes for questions ( f q ) and passages ( f d ), trained contrastively on (question, gold-passage) pairs. The 49 ◦ principal angle between query and document subspaces (measured on 2WikiMultiHopQA) is by design: it maximizes retrieval discriminability. We exploit this geometry: encoding a full bridge passage as a query ( f q ( B ) ) is out-of-distribution for the encoder and is, on aggregate, worse than encoding the question itself ( f q ( q ) ), though this aggregate masks a regime split. For Q-dominant queries (P1=True), the question already names the hop2 entity and f q ( q ) dominates. For B-dominant queries (P1=False), the relation-bearing sentence B rel inside the bridge explicitly names the hop-2 entity, and fusing f q ( B rel ) with f q ( q ) corrects the ranking.
texts.text: Asymmetric bi-encoders. Modern retrievers like NV-Embed-v2 (Lee et al., 2024), BGElarge (Xiao et al., 2023), and e5-mistral (Wang et al., 2024) use separate encoder modes for questions ( f q ) and passages ( f d ), trained contrastively on (question, gold-passage) pairs. The 49 ◦ principal angle between query and document subspaces (measured on 2WikiMultiHopQA) is by design: it maximizes retrieval discriminability. We exploit this geometry: encoding a full bridge passage as a query ( f q ( B ) ) is out-of-distribution for the encoder and is, on aggregate, worse than encoding the question itself ( f q ( q ) ), though this aggregate masks a regime split. For Q-dominant queries (P1=True), the question already names the hop2 entity and f q ( q ) dominates. For B-dominant queries (P1=False), the relation-bearing sentence B rel inside the bridge explicitly names the hop-2 entity, and fusing f q ( B rel ) with f q ( q ) corrects the ranking.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Existing work and its assumption. IRCOT (Trivedi et al., 2023) reformulates the bridge as a query for hop-2 retrieval; HIPPORAG2 (Guti´ errez et al., 2025) uses PPR over a bridge-passage graph; PROPRAG (Wang and Han, 2025) does beam search over extracted propositions. All assume the bridge passage is the right signal for hop-2. We show this is regime-dependent: true for B-dominant queries (P1=False, P2=True), harmful for Q-dominant queries (P1=True).
texts.text: Existing work and its assumption. IRCOT (Trivedi et al., 2023) reformulates the bridge as a query for hop-2 retrieval; HIPPORAG2 (Guti´ errez et al., 2025) uses PPR over a bridge-passage graph; PROPRAG (Wang and Han, 2025) does beam search over extracted propositions. All assume the bridge passage is the right signal for hop-2. We show this is regime-dependent: true for B-dominant queries (P1=False, P2=True), harmful for Q-dominant queries (P1=True).

Compositional and inference queries, where the bridge identifies the hop-2 entity by name, show ∆ < 0 : the bridge embedding outperforms the question.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Empirical support (Test 4 / Theorem 2): AUC flips sign precisely at the P1 boundary ( p < 0 . 001 across all three datasets and three encoders). Type labels ('comparison', 'compositional') are a proxy for P1/P2, not the causal mechanism: swapping the bridge text of a bridge comparison query with that of a compositional query shifts performance from B-dominant to Q-dominant ( 0 . 962 → 0 . 485 , p< 0 . 001 ; Test 3).
texts.text: Empirical support (Test 4 / Theorem 2): AUC flips sign precisely at the P1 boundary ( p < 0 . 001 across all three datasets and three encoders). Type labels ('comparison', 'compositional') are a proxy for P1/P2, not the causal mechanism: swapping the bridge text of a bridge comparison query with that of a compositional query shifts performance from B-dominant to Q-dominant ( 0 . 962 → 0 . 485 , p< 0 . 001 ; Test 3).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4.1 Theorem 1: AUC as a Function of Separation Margin
texts.text: 4.1 Theorem 1: AUC as a Function of Separation Margin

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Theorem 1 (Separation-AUC Calibration) . Let S i = f q ( x ) ⊤ f d ( g i ) -E d ∼P [ f q ( x ) ⊤ f d ( d )] be the score separation margin for query i (gold score minus pool mean). Under the assumption that pool scores are approximately Gaussian, perquery AUC satisfies:
texts.text: Theorem 1 (Separation-AUC Calibration) . Let S i = f q ( x ) ⊤ f d ( g i ) -E d ∼P [ f q ( x ) ⊤ f d ( d )] be the score separation margin for query i (gold score minus pool mean). Under the assumption that pool scores are approximately Gaussian, perquery AUC satisfies:

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: AUC i ( x ) ≈ Φ ( S i σ P ) (1)

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where Φ is the standard normal CDF and σ P is the pool score standard deviation. Consequently, AUC i is a monotone function of S i : larger margin implies higher per-query AUC.
texts.text: where Φ is the standard normal CDF and σ P is the pool score standard deviation. Consequently, AUC i is a monotone function of S i : larger margin implies higher per-query AUC.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Validation. We measure S i on all hop-1-correct queries and fit Φ( z ) to the empirical AUC i vs. S i scatter. R 2 ≥ 0 . 90 for six of eight type × encoder combinations; inversion accuracy (sign of predicted ∆ AUC matches observed) is 100% for compositional and bridge comparison types (Figure 1). The Cantelli inequality provides a nonparametric lower bound: P (AUC > t ) ≥ 1 / (1 + ( t -0 . 5) 2 /σ 2 ) , validated at p = 1 . 4 × 10 -19 for bridge comparison (Figure 3).
texts.text: Validation. We measure S i on all hop-1-correct queries and fit Φ( z ) to the empirical AUC i vs. S i scatter. R 2 ≥ 0 . 90 for six of eight type × encoder combinations; inversion accuracy (sign of predicted ∆ AUC matches observed) is 100% for compositional and bridge comparison types (Figure 1). The Cantelli inequality provides a nonparametric lower bound: P (AUC > t ) ≥ 1 / (1 + ( t -0 . 5) 2 /σ 2 ) , validated at p = 1 . 4 × 10 -19 for bridge comparison (Figure 3).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Theorem 2 (Regime Decomposition) . The retrieval regime of a two-hop query is determined by the sign of ∆ S = E [ S b -S q ] , where S q ( S b ) is the score separation margin for the question (bridge) embedding. P1 is decisive for routing: ∆ S < 0 (Q-dominant) when P1=True; when P1=False, P2=True identifies the B-dominant case ( ∆ S > 0 ). This holds across NV-Embed-v2, BGE-largeen-v1.5, and e5-mistral-7b on all three datasets.
texts.text: Theorem 2 (Regime Decomposition) . The retrieval regime of a two-hop query is determined by the sign of ∆ S = E [ S b -S q ] , where S q ( S b ) is the score separation margin for the question (bridge) embedding. P1 is decisive for routing: ∆ S < 0 (Q-dominant) when P1=True; when P1=False, P2=True identifies the B-dominant case ( ∆ S > 0 ). This holds across NV-Embed-v2, BGE-largeen-v1.5, and e5-mistral-7b on all three datasets.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Validation. We operationalize P1 as hop2 titlein-question : the hop-2 gold passage title appears as a substring of the question text. Regime assignment via this proxy matches AUC-derived regime labels with accuracy > 95% on all three datasets. Encoder replication (Test 5 / H1): Kendall τ between predicted and observed AUC ordering is significant for all eight type × encoder pairs (Figure 4).
texts.text: Validation. We operationalize P1 as hop2 titlein-question : the hop-2 gold passage title appears as a substring of the question text. Regime assignment via this proxy matches AUC-derived regime labels with accuracy > 95% on all three datasets. Encoder replication (Test 5 / H1): Kendall τ between predicted and observed AUC ordering is significant for all eight type × encoder pairs (Figure 4).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Mixture reversal (Corollary 1). Because comparison queries (P1=True, approximately 48% of 2WikiMultiHopQA) are Q-dominant with large ∆ = +0 . 650 , they dominate the aggregate AUC statistics (Figure 2). The aggregate ∆ = +0 . 314 masks the B-dominant regime of compositional and inference queries ( ∆ < 0 ). Type-stratified or predicate-stratified analysis is required.
texts.text: Mixture reversal (Corollary 1). Because comparison queries (P1=True, approximately 48% of 2WikiMultiHopQA) are Q-dominant with large ∆ = +0 . 650 , they dominate the aggregate AUC statistics (Figure 2). The aggregate ∆ = +0 . 314 masks the B-dominant regime of compositional and inference queries ( ∆ < 0 ). Type-stratified or predicate-stratified analysis is required.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Corollary 1 (Mixture Reversal) . When Qdominant query types have high prevalence in a dataset, aggregate retrieval statistics reverse the within-regime law. Specifically, aggregate AUC(q) > AUC(B) can coexist with per-type AUC(q) < AUC(B) for B-dominant types. This is not a dataset artifact: the reversal is produced by the P1/P2 distribution of the dataset, not by any property of the encoder.
texts.text: Corollary 1 (Mixture Reversal) . When Qdominant query types have high prevalence in a dataset, aggregate retrieval statistics reverse the within-regime law. Specifically, aggregate AUC(q) > AUC(B) can coexist with per-type AUC(q) < AUC(B) for B-dominant types. This is not a dataset artifact: the reversal is produced by the P1/P2 distribution of the dataset, not by any property of the encoder.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Theorem 3 (Relational Composition) . The bridge advantage for B-dominant queries comes from the relation-bearing sentence B rel ⊂ b , not from entity name occurrence alone. Formally:
texts.text: Theorem 3 (Relational Composition) . The bridge advantage for B-dominant queries comes from the relation-bearing sentence B rel ⊂ b , not from entity name occurrence alone. Formally:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. Ret( q, b ) ≈ Ret( q, B rel ) : full bridge and relational sentence give equivalent retrieval performance.
texts.text: Ret( q, b ) ≈ Ret( q, B rel ) : full bridge and relational sentence give equivalent retrieval performance.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. Ret( q, b \ B rel ) ≪ Ret( q, b ) : removing B rel from the bridge collapses performance.
texts.text: Ret( q, b \ B rel ) ≪ Ret( q, b ) : removing B rel from the bridge collapses performance.

texts.text: Figure 2: Mixture reversal in 2WikiMultiHopQA (Corollary 1). Aggregate ∆ AUC (gray bar, ∆ = -0 . 369 ) is negative, masking the per-type inversion: comparison and bridge-comp are Q-dominant ( ∆ < 0 ); compositional and inference are B-dominant ( ∆ > 0 ).

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 3. B rel = fi rst sentence in general: the entityname sentence alone is insufficient; structural heuristics miss cases where the relation verb is critical for identifying e 2 .
texts.text: B rel = fi rst sentence in general: the entityname sentence alone is insufficient; structural heuristics miss cases where the relation verb is critical for identifying e 2 .

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Validation (bridge knockout experiment). We compare three bridge variants: b (full bridge), B rel (selected relation-bearing sentence), and b \ B rel (bridge minus B rel). Using e5-mistral-7b (Nebius API, n = 300 , concurrent 3-min runtime): B rel ≈ B full ( ∆ ≈ 0 , ns), B minus rel collapses ( ∆ = -0 . 086 to -0 . 141 , p< 0 . 001 ). Human annotation ( κ = 1 . 00 (Cohen, 1960), n = 50 doubly annotated) confirms that B rel is structurally identifiable (relation verb present, entity present, propositional content present): it is not defined circularly.
texts.text: Validation (bridge knockout experiment). We compare three bridge variants: b (full bridge), B rel (selected relation-bearing sentence), and b \ B rel (bridge minus B rel). Using e5-mistral-7b (Nebius API, n = 300 , concurrent 3-min runtime): B rel ≈ B full ( ∆ ≈ 0 , ns), B minus rel collapses ( ∆ = -0 . 086 to -0 . 141 , p< 0 . 001 ). Human annotation ( κ = 1 . 00 (Cohen, 1960), n = 50 doubly annotated) confirms that B rel is structurally identifiable (relation verb present, entity present, propositional content present): it is not defined circularly.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Figure 3: Bridge score separation margin S b by query type. Q-dominant types (comparison, bridge-comp; µ < 0 ): bridge embedding ranks the gold passage below the pool mean. B-dominant types (compositional, inference; µ > 0 ): bridge separates the gold passage above the pool mean. Cantelli bound validated at p = 1 . 4 × 10 -19 for bridge comparison.
texts.text: Figure 3: Bridge score separation margin S b by query type. Q-dominant types (comparison, bridge-comp; µ < 0 ): bridge embedding ranks the gold passage below the pool mean. B-dominant types (compositional, inference; µ > 0 ): bridge separates the gold passage above the pool mean. Cantelli bound validated at p = 1 . 4 × 10 -19 for bridge comparison.

texts.orig: Given a question q and bridge passage b , REGIMEROUTER selects one of two retrieval actions:
texts.text: Given a question q and bridge passage b , REGIMEROUTER selects one of two retrieval actions:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Q : rank the candidate pool by f q ( q ) ⊤ f d ( c )
texts.text: Q : rank the candidate pool by f q ( q ) ⊤ f d ( c )

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: · Union : rank by (1 -α ) · f q ( q ) ⊤ f d ( c ) + α · f q ( B rel ) ⊤ f d ( c )
texts.text: Union : rank by (1 -α ) · f q ( q ) ⊤ f d ( c ) + α · f q ( B rel ) ⊤ f d ( c )

texts.label: text
texts.orig: where B rel is the relation-bearing sentence selected by a learned sentence selector (Section 5.2).
texts.text: where B rel is the relation-bearing sentence selected by a learned sentence selector (Section 5.2).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The routing decision is made by a logistic regression classifier trained on five features derived from P1/P2 proxy definitions:
texts.text: The routing decision is made by a logistic regression classifier trained on five features derived from P1/P2 proxy definitions:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. q comparison word : whether the question contains a comparison word ( differ, same, versus, whereas, etc.), a proxy for P1=True
texts.text: q comparison word : whether the question contains a comparison word ( differ, same, versus, whereas, etc.), a proxy for P1=True

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. q ynstart : whether the question starts with a yes/no verb ( did, was, were, etc.), a proxy for P1=True
texts.text: q ynstart : whether the question starts with a yes/no verb ( did, was, were, etc.), a proxy for P1=True

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 3. q entity count : number of proper nouns in q , a proxy for P1 strength
texts.text: q entity count : number of proper nouns in q , a proxy for P1 strength

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 4. b new entity count : proper nouns in B rel not in q , a proxy for P2=True (novel entities introduced by bridge)
texts.text: b new entity count : proper nouns in B rel not in q , a proxy for P2=True (novel entities introduced by bridge)

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 5. b rel frac : | B rel | / | b | , a proxy for bridge informativeness
texts.text: b rel frac : | B rel | / | b | , a proxy for bridge informativeness

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The label for training is: Union improves over Q on this query (computed from embedding scores, requiring no LLM call). Training is fully selfsupervised from the retrieval evaluation itself.
texts.text: The label for training is: Union improves over Q on this query (computed from embedding scores, requiring no LLM call). Training is fully selfsupervised from the retrieval evaluation itself.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The sentence selector is a logistic regression classifier trained on 100 human-annotated pairs (2WikiMultiHopQA, annotation set H2) to identify which sentence in a bridge passage is the relation-bearing sentence. Features are: new entity count, presence of a relation verb, position fraction, sentence length, and named entity density.
texts.text: The sentence selector is a logistic regression classifier trained on 100 human-annotated pairs (2WikiMultiHopQA, annotation set H2) to identify which sentence in a bridge passage is the relation-bearing sentence. Features are: new entity count, presence of a relation verb, position fraction, sentence length, and named entity density.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The selector identifies B rel with 56.7% oracle accuracy on the annotated set. The bottleneck is structural: 56.2% of 2WikiMultiHopQA queries have no sentence in the bridge that verbatim mentions the hop-2 title (the oracle definition). For these queries the router correctly falls back to Qonly.
texts.text: The selector identifies B rel with 56.7% oracle accuracy on the annotated set. The bottleneck is structural: 56.2% of 2WikiMultiHopQA queries have no sentence in the bridge that verbatim mentions the hop-2 title (the oracle definition). For these queries the router correctly falls back to Qonly.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: fold cross-fitting with KFold(n splits=5, shuffle=False) . Labels and features are derived from embedding scores and surface text only. No oracle labels are used at training time. The full classifier (trained on all 881 examples) is used for zero-shot transfer.
texts.text: fold cross-fitting with KFold(n splits=5, shuffle=False) . Labels and features are derived from embedding scores and surface text only. No oracle labels are used at training time. The full classifier (trained on all 881 examples) is used for zero-shot transfer.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Deployment rule : all reported main results use a single frozen α = 0 . 25 across all three datasets.
texts.text: Deployment rule : all reported main results use a single frozen α = 0 . 25 across all three datasets.

texts.label: code
texts.orig: Require: Question q , bridge passage b , candidate pool P 1: B rel ← SENTSELECTOR ( b, q ) 2: x ← FEATURES ( q, B rel , b ) 3: ˆ a ← BINARYLR.PREDICT ( x ) ∈ { Q , Union } 4: α ← 0 . 25 { single frozen value across all domains } 5: if ˆ a = Union then 6: score ( c ) ← (1 - α ) · f q ( q ) ⊤ f d ( c )+ α · f q ( B rel ) ⊤ f d ( c ) 7: else 8: score ( c ) ← f q ( q ) ⊤ f d ( c ) 9: end if 10: return top- k passages by score ( c )
texts.text: Require: Question q , bridge passage b , candidate pool P 1: B rel ← SENTSELECTOR ( b, q ) 2: x ← FEATURES ( q, B rel , b ) 3: ˆ a ← BINARYLR.PREDICT ( x ) ∈ { Q , Union } 4: α ← 0 . 25 { single frozen value across all domains } 5: if ˆ a = Union then 6: score ( c ) ← (1 - α ) · f q ( q ) ⊤ f d ( c )+ α · f q ( B rel ) ⊤ f d ( c ) 7: else 8: score ( c ) ← f q ( q ) ⊤ f d ( c ) 9: end if 10: return top- k passages by score ( c )

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This conservative value is robust across domains: P-weighted α and higher α = 0 . 5 improve indomain performance (see ablation, Table 4) but degrade zero-shot due to cross-domain miscalibration. The ablation reports the α = 0 . 5 in-domain upper bound separately.
texts.text: This conservative value is robust across domains: P-weighted α and higher α = 0 . 5 improve indomain performance (see ablation, Table 4) but degrade zero-shot due to cross-domain miscalibration. The ablation reports the α = 0 . 5 in-domain upper bound separately.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Cost : approximately $1 . 2 µ $/query for B rel embedding (Nebius API); latency ∼ 100 ms (parallelizable with q embedding). The router classifier and sentence selector are CPU-only ( < 1 ms inference).
texts.text: Cost : approximately $1 . 2 µ $/query for B rel embedding (Nebius API); latency ∼ 100 ms (parallelizable with q embedding). The router classifier and sentence selector are CPU-only ( < 1 ms inference).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We evaluate on 2WikiMultiHopQA (Ho et al., 2020) (2Wiki; n = 881 bridge-type queries), MuSiQue (Trivedi et al., 2022) (n = 303 bridgetype queries), and HotpotQA (Yang et al., 2018) (n = 570 bridge-type) using the HippoRAG2 processed corpora. We use BGE-large-en-v1.5 for query and document embeddings (pool construction) and e5-mistral-7b for B rel embedding (Nebius API). Hop-1 retrieval uses NV-Embed-v2; we evaluate only on hop-1-correct queries (bridge in top-5). The metric is R @5 on the hop-2 gold passage; significance is McNemar's test (McNemar, 1947) on paired binary outcomes.
texts.text: We evaluate on 2WikiMultiHopQA (Ho et al., 2020) (2Wiki; n = 881 bridge-type queries), MuSiQue (Trivedi et al., 2022) (n = 303 bridgetype queries), and HotpotQA (Yang et al., 2018) (n = 570 bridge-type) using the HippoRAG2 processed corpora. We use BGE-large-en-v1.5 for query and document embeddings (pool construction) and e5-mistral-7b for B rel embedding (Nebius API). Hop-1 retrieval uses NV-Embed-v2; we evaluate only on hop-1-correct queries (bridge in top-5). The metric is R @5 on the hop-2 gold passage; significance is McNemar's test (McNemar, 1947) on paired binary outcomes.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Table 2 shows the main results. On 2Wiki (training domain), REGIMEROUTER improves R @5 by 5.6 pp ( p < 0 . 001 , 54W/5L). Zero-shot transfer to MuSiQue, a homogeneous B-dominant dataset (P1=0%, P2=100%), achieves 5.3 pp improvement ( p = 0 . 002 , 22W/6L). HotpotQA is a near-ceiling Q-dominant dataset ( R @5 = 0 . 856 Q-only, ∆( Union oracle ) = +2 . 5 pp), and REGIMEROUTER achieves a positive 1.1 pp trend (14W/8L, p = 0 . 143 ) with no harm in a regime where routing provides limited upside.
texts.text: Table 2 shows the main results. On 2Wiki (training domain), REGIMEROUTER improves R @5 by 5.6 pp ( p < 0 . 001 , 54W/5L). Zero-shot transfer to MuSiQue, a homogeneous B-dominant dataset (P1=0%, P2=100%), achieves 5.3 pp improvement ( p = 0 . 002 , 22W/6L). HotpotQA is a near-ceiling Q-dominant dataset ( R @5 = 0 . 856 Q-only, ∆( Union oracle ) = +2 . 5 pp), and REGIMEROUTER achieves a positive 1.1 pp trend (14W/8L, p = 0 . 143 ) with no harm in a regime where routing provides limited upside.

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Table 2: REGIMEROUTER results across three datasets. Training on 2Wiki (5-fold cross-fitted); zero-shot transfer to MuSiQue and HotpotQA. Baseline = Q-only retrieval. Gains are significant where B-dominant regime queries are prevalent (2Wiki, MuSiQue); HotpotQA is a near-ceiling Q-dominant corpus ( R @5 = 0 . 856 baseline, p = 0 . 143 positive trend).
texts.text: Table 2: REGIMEROUTER results across three datasets. Training on 2Wiki (5-fold cross-fitted); zero-shot transfer to MuSiQue and HotpotQA. Baseline = Q-only retrieval. Gains are significant where B-dominant regime queries are prevalent (2Wiki, MuSiQue); HotpotQA is a near-ceiling Q-dominant corpus ( R @5 = 0 . 856 baseline, p = 0 . 143 positive trend).

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Table 3: Oracle analysis decomposing the performance gap.
texts.text: Table 3: Oracle analysis decomposing the performance gap.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: No-regret pattern. The three-dataset pattern is consistent with theory: significant gain where Bdominant queries are present (2Wiki with mixed regime, MuSiQue with pure B-dominant), positive non-significant trend where near-ceiling Qdominant (HotpotQA). The error ratio (W/L) is 10.8:1 on 2Wiki, 3.7:1 on MuSiQue, 1.75:1 on HotpotQA.
texts.text: No-regret pattern. The three-dataset pattern is consistent with theory: significant gain where Bdominant queries are present (2Wiki with mixed regime, MuSiQue with pure B-dominant), positive non-significant trend where near-ceiling Qdominant (HotpotQA). The error ratio (W/L) is 10.8:1 on 2Wiki, 3.7:1 on MuSiQue, 1.75:1 on HotpotQA.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Table 3 decomposes the remaining gap. The oracle router (knowing the true optimal action for each query) achieves +11 . 7 pp, confirming substantial headroom. The gap to the oracle router is +4 . 6 pp (routing accuracy) vs +0 . 2 pp (selector precision from oracle B rel labels), confirming that the bottleneck is routing accuracy, not sentence extraction. The structural coverage limit (56.2% of queries have no oracle sentence) is a fundamental constraint on extraction-based approaches.
texts.text: Table 3 decomposes the remaining gap. The oracle router (knowing the true optimal action for each query) achieves +11 . 7 pp, confirming substantial headroom. The gap to the oracle router is +4 . 6 pp (routing accuracy) vs +0 . 2 pp (selector precision from oracle B rel labels), confirming that the bottleneck is routing accuracy, not sentence extraction. The structural coverage limit (56.2% of queries have no oracle sentence) is a fundamental constraint on extraction-based approaches.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Table 4 shows four distinct contributions: (1) using the full bridge adds 2.8 pp, confirming that bridge content is useful even without relation selection; (2) selecting B rel without routing adds 5.1 pp, confirming that sentence selection captures most of the bridge signal; (3) routing (applying Union only when the router predicts it is beneficial) adds a further 1.5 pp over no-routing; (4) α = 0 . 5 in-domain outperforms α = 0 . 25 , confirming that the halving rule has a small cost indomain but is critical for zero-shot robustness.
texts.text: Table 4 shows four distinct contributions: (1) using the full bridge adds 2.8 pp, confirming that bridge content is useful even without relation selection; (2) selecting B rel without routing adds 5.1 pp, confirming that sentence selection captures most of the bridge signal; (3) routing (applying Union only when the router predicts it is beneficial) adds a further 1.5 pp over no-routing; (4) α = 0 . 5 in-domain outperforms α = 0 . 25 , confirming that the halving rule has a small cost indomain but is critical for zero-shot robustness.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The NE heuristic router (using entity counts directly without logistic regression) achieves only +2.1 pp ( p = 0 . 063 , ns), confirming that the learned combination of features is necessary.
texts.text: The NE heuristic router (using entity counts directly without logistic regression) achieves only +2.1 pp ( p = 0 . 063 , ns), confirming that the learned combination of features is necessary.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Confidence threshold sweep. Varying the routing threshold from 0.5 to 0.75 monotonically reduces 2Wiki R @5 (from 0.898 to 0.854), confirming τ = 0 . 5 is already optimal. At τ = 0 . 5 , 35.9% of queries are routed to Union; raising the threshold increasingly selects only the easiest Union cases, reducing coverage without improving precision.
texts.text: Confidence threshold sweep. Varying the routing threshold from 0.5 to 0.75 monotonically reduces 2Wiki R @5 (from 0.898 to 0.854), confirming τ = 0 . 5 is already optimal. At τ = 0 . 5 , 35.9% of queries are routed to Union; raising the threshold increasingly selects only the easiest Union cases, reducing coverage without improving precision.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: P-weighted α . Using α q = clip ( ˆ P ( Union ) × 0 . 5 , 0 . 1 , 0 . 5) instead of the fixed rule improves 2Wiki to +6 . 6 pp (+1.0 pp over fixed) but degrades MuSiQue to +2 . 6 pp (ns) and HotpotQA to -0 . 2 pp. The router is well-calibrated in-domain but assigns systematically lower ˆ P ( Union ) to MuSiQue queries (all B-dominant, differing in distribution from mixed 2Wiki training), so adaptive weighting clips to α ≈ 0 . 1 -0 . 2 instead of the optimal 0.25. This confirms that fi xed conservative α = 0 . 25 is the robust zero-shot policy .
texts.text: P-weighted α . Using α q = clip ( ˆ P ( Union ) × 0 . 5 , 0 . 1 , 0 . 5) instead of the fixed rule improves 2Wiki to +6 . 6 pp (+1.0 pp over fixed) but degrades MuSiQue to +2 . 6 pp (ns) and HotpotQA to -0 . 2 pp. The router is well-calibrated in-domain but assigns systematically lower ˆ P ( Union ) to MuSiQue queries (all B-dominant, differing in distribution from mixed 2Wiki training), so adaptive weighting clips to α ≈ 0 . 1 -0 . 2 instead of the optimal 0.25. This confirms that fi xed conservative α = 0 . 25 is the robust zero-shot policy .

texts.orig: Q-dominant (comparison types) and B-dominant (compositional/inference) hold across both encoders
texts.text: Q-dominant (comparison types) and B-dominant (compositional/inference) hold across both encoders

texts.label: caption
texts.orig: Figure 4: Regime AUC pattern for NV-Embed-v2 and BGE-large-en-v1.5 (plus e5-mistral-7b, not shown; results consistent). AUC(Q) dominates for comparison and bridge-comp (Q-dominant); AUC(B) dominates for compositional and inference (B-dominant). The ordering is encoder-agnostic; Kendall τ is significant across all type × encoder pairs ( p < 0 . 01 ).
texts.text: Figure 4: Regime AUC pattern for NV-Embed-v2 and BGE-large-en-v1.5 (plus e5-mistral-7b, not shown; results consistent). AUC(Q) dominates for comparison and bridge-comp (Q-dominant); AUC(B) dominates for compositional and inference (B-dominant). The ordering is encoder-agnostic; Kendall τ is significant across all type × encoder pairs ( p < 0 . 01 ).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Wereplicate the regime pattern across NV-Embedv2 (Nebius API), BGE-large-en-v1.5 (local), and e5-mistral-7b (Nebius API, n = 300 , 3-minute concurrent run). Kendall τ between predicted and observed AUC ordering is significant for all eight type × encoder combinations ( p < 0 . 01 ). The Bdominant → Q-dominant AUC ordering is identical across encoders, confirming that the regime is determined by query structure, not encoderspecific geometry (Figure 4).
texts.text: Wereplicate the regime pattern across NV-Embedv2 (Nebius API), BGE-large-en-v1.5 (local), and e5-mistral-7b (Nebius API, n = 300 , 3-minute concurrent run). Kendall τ between predicted and observed AUC ordering is significant for all eight type × encoder combinations ( p < 0 . 01 ). The Bdominant → Q-dominant AUC ordering is identical across encoders, confirming that the regime is determined by query structure, not encoderspecific geometry (Figure 4).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Iterative retrieval. IRCOT (Trivedi et al., 2023) generates bridge-conditioned hop-2 queries using an LLM, assuming bridge re-encoding helps universally. Our work shows this is regimedependent: for P1=True queries, bridge reencoding adds noise.
texts.text: Iterative retrieval. IRCOT (Trivedi et al., 2023) generates bridge-conditioned hop-2 queries using an LLM, assuming bridge re-encoding helps universally. Our work shows this is regimedependent: for P1=True queries, bridge reencoding adds noise.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Graph-structured retrieval. HIPPORAG2 (Guti´ errez et al., 2025) and PROPRAG (Wang and Han, 2025) build knowledge graphs at index time and traverse them at query time, encoding bridge-linked entity structure before any query is seen. REGIMEROUTER achieves comparable retrieval improvement without index-time LLM cost by routing from surface-text features instead.
texts.text: Graph-structured retrieval. HIPPORAG2 (Guti´ errez et al., 2025) and PROPRAG (Wang and Han, 2025) build knowledge graphs at index time and traverse them at query time, encoding bridge-linked entity structure before any query is seen. REGIMEROUTER achieves comparable retrieval improvement without index-time LLM cost by routing from surface-text features instead.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Query routing. Adaptive retrieval approaches select between retrieval systems or generation models based on query difficulty or type. We in- stead route between retrieval strategies within a single system, deriving routing features from retrieval theory rather than difficulty proxies.
texts.text: Query routing. Adaptive retrieval approaches select between retrieval systems or generation models based on query difficulty or type. We in- stead route between retrieval strategies within a single system, deriving routing features from retrieval theory rather than difficulty proxies.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: BRIDGERAG (Bacellar, 2024) implements conditional scoring s ( q, b, c ) via an LLM judge, achieving R @5 = 0 . 8316 on MuSiQue (n=1000). REGIMEROUTER achieves +5 . 3 pp zero-shot on MuSiQue without any LLM at query time , using only the relation-bearing sentence as a lightweight bridge signal. Note that REGIMEROUTER's MuSiQue result uses the n = 303 cross-dataset evaluation slice, while BRIDGERAG reports on the full 1000 queries; the comparison is directional. The remaining performance gap reflects the irreducible value of full semantic reasoning over ( q, b, c ) triples.
texts.text: BRIDGERAG (Bacellar, 2024) implements conditional scoring s ( q, b, c ) via an LLM judge, achieving R @5 = 0 . 8316 on MuSiQue (n=1000). REGIMEROUTER achieves +5 . 3 pp zero-shot on MuSiQue without any LLM at query time , using only the relation-bearing sentence as a lightweight bridge signal. Note that REGIMEROUTER's MuSiQue result uses the n = 303 cross-dataset evaluation slice, while BRIDGERAG reports on the full 1000 queries; the comparison is directional. The remaining performance gap reflects the irreducible value of full semantic reasoning over ( q, b, c ) triples.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: We have shown that two-hop QA retrieval divides into Q-dominant and B-dominant regimes, determined by whether the hop-2 target entity is named in the question. Three theorems formalize this structure: AUC calibrates to cosine separation margin (T1); P1 is decisive for routing and P2 qualifies the B-dominant case, with both predicates derivable from surface text and validated across encoders and datasets (T2); and bridge advantage requires the relation-bearing sentence specifically, not entity occurrence alone (T3).
texts.text: We have shown that two-hop QA retrieval divides into Q-dominant and B-dominant regimes, determined by whether the hop-2 target entity is named in the question. Three theorems formalize this structure: AUC calibrates to cosine separation margin (T1); P1 is decisive for routing and P2 qualifies the B-dominant case, with both predicates derivable from surface text and validated across encoders and datasets (T2); and bridge advantage requires the relation-bearing sentence specifically, not entity occurrence alone (T3).

texts.label: text
texts.orig: REGIMEROUTER operationalizes this theory as a five-feature binary router with a single frozen de- ployment rule ( α = 0 . 25 across all three datasets; α = 0 . 5 is reported separately as an in-domain ablation upper bound). The result is a crossdataset no-regret system: +5 . 6 pp ( p< 0 . 001 ) indomain on 2Wiki, +5 . 3 pp ( p =0 . 002 ) zero-shot on MuSiQue (pure B-dominant), and a no-regret +1 . 1 pp on HotpotQA (near-ceiling Q-dominant, p = 0 . 143 ). Training uses 881 embedding scores for regime labels and 100 human-annotated sentences for the selector; no LLM is invoked at query time.
texts.text: REGIMEROUTER operationalizes this theory as a five-feature binary router with a single frozen de- ployment rule ( α = 0 . 25 across all three datasets; α = 0 . 5 is reported separately as an in-domain ablation upper bound). The result is a crossdataset no-regret system: +5 . 6 pp ( p< 0 . 001 ) indomain on 2Wiki, +5 . 3 pp ( p =0 . 002 ) zero-shot on MuSiQue (pure B-dominant), and a no-regret +1 . 1 pp on HotpotQA (near-ceiling Q-dominant, p = 0 . 143 ). Training uses 881 embedding scores for regime labels and 100 human-annotated sentences for the selector; no LLM is invoked at query time.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The principal finding with practical consequence is that the routing bottleneck is not feature engineering ( +0 . 2 pp from oracle selector) but routing accuracy ( +4 . 6 pp to oracle router). Improving the learned routing policy, for example through calibrated confidence or domain-adaptive features, is the primary path to closing the gap to oracle performance.
texts.text: The principal finding with practical consequence is that the routing bottleneck is not feature engineering ( +0 . 2 pp from oracle selector) but routing accuracy ( +4 . 6 pp to oracle router). Improving the learned routing policy, for example through calibrated confidence or domain-adaptive features, is the primary path to closing the gap to oracle performance.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The theorems are formalized for two-hop compositional benchmarks where questions are constructed by composing single-hop questions, making P1 and P2 binary and well-defined. The regime theory may not directly extend to: (1) questions with implicit rather than explicit compositional structure; (2) multi-hop chains beyond two hops; (3) encoders trained with bridge conditioning. The router is trained on 2WikiMultiHopQA and zero-shot applied to MuSiQue and HotpotQA; further evaluation on additional domains is needed.
texts.text: The theorems are formalized for two-hop compositional benchmarks where questions are constructed by composing single-hop questions, making P1 and P2 binary and well-defined. The regime theory may not directly extend to: (1) questions with implicit rather than explicit compositional structure; (2) multi-hop chains beyond two hops; (3) encoders trained with bridge conditioning. The router is trained on 2WikiMultiHopQA and zero-shot applied to MuSiQue and HotpotQA; further evaluation on additional domains is needed.
